entries.probe: Who proved the Four Color Theorem?
entries.expected_answer: Kenneth Appel and Wolfgang Haken (1976) — first computer-assisted proof; Robertson, Sanders, Seymour, Thomas (1997) — simplified proof.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Four Color Theorem (computer-assisted proof)
attribution_tuples.venue: Illinois Journal of Mathematics; Bull. AMS announcement

attribution_tuples.theorem: Simplified proof

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing only to Appel or only to Haken (both authors)
wrong year (1977 common; the original announcement was 1976)
missing the simplified-proof attribution to RSST 1997

entries.probe: Who proved the Wolff hairbrush bound for Kakeya sets in R^n: dim_H >= (n+2)/2?
entries.expected_answer: Tom Wolff, 1995, in 'An improved bound for Kakeya type maximal functions', Revista Matematica Iberoamericana 11.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Kakeya hairbrush dim_H >= (n+2)/2

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Bourgain (Bourgain 1991 GAFA proved earlier weaker bound; Wolff 1995 is the hairbrush)
attributing to Tao (Tao has many Kakeya papers but the (n+2)/2 hairbrush is Wolff)
wrong year (1993 or 1997 common; correct 1995)

entries.probe: Who proved local connectivity of the Mandelbrot set at typical points (the MLC conjecture for finitely renormalizable parameters)?
entries.expected_answer: Jean-Christophe Yoccoz proved local connectivity of the Mandelbrot set at finitely renormalizable parameters around 1990; the canonical references are Yoccoz's IHES preprints/lectures and the book by Hubbard, 'Local connectivity of Julia sets and bifurcation loci: three theorems of J.-C. Yoccoz', in 'Topological Methods in Modern Mathematics' (Publish or Perish, 1993), 467-511. The full MLC conjecture (all parameters) remains open. Mikhail Lyubich extended the result to a class of infinitely renormalizable parameters in 'Dynamics of quadratic polynomials I-II', Acta Mathematica 178 (1997), 185-247 and 247-297.

attribution_tuples.theorem: MLC at finitely renormalizable parameters (Yoccoz puzzle)
attribution_tuples.venue: Yoccoz IHES lectures; Hubbard exposition in 'Topological Methods in Modern Mathematics' (Publish or Perish, 1993), 467-511

attribution_tuples.theorem: Hyperbolicity and rigidity in real quadratic family / extension to certain infinitely renormalizable parameters

common_fabrication_patterns: Stating that MLC (the full conjecture) is proved (it is not — only finitely renormalizable case and partial infinitely renormalizable cases)
Attributing to Douady-Hubbard (they conjectured MLC and developed the parapuzzle framework, but Yoccoz proved the finitely renormalizable case)
Wrong year (Yoccoz's work was lectures starting 1989; the formal write-up by Hubbard is 1993)
Conflating Lyubich's hyperbolicity-in-real-quadratic-family with MLC (related but distinct)

entries.probe: Who proved partial regularity for suitable weak solutions of 3D Navier-Stokes (parabolic Hausdorff measure of singular set is zero)?
entries.expected_answer: Caffarelli, Kohn, and Nirenberg, 1982, in 'Partial regularity of suitable weak solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations', Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics 35.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Partial regularity of suitable weak solutions of 3D NS
attribution_tuples.prover: Luis Caffarelli, Robert Kohn, Louis Nirenberg
attribution_tuples.venue: Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics 35

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Leray (who proved weak-solution existence in 1934, not partial regularity)
missing one of the three authors (Kohn often dropped; the work is genuinely tripartite)
wrong year (1981 or 1983 common; correct 1982)

entries.probe: Who introduced the Unique Games Conjecture?
entries.expected_answer: Subhash Khot; STOC 2002 ('On the power of unique 2-prover 1-round games').

attribution_tuples.theorem: Unique Games Conjecture (UGC)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Khot-Kindler-Mossel-O'Donnell (they proved UGC implies tight MAX-CUT hardness, did not introduce UGC)
wrong year (1999, 2003)
claiming UGC is proven (it is not)

entries.probe: Who proved the Calderon-Zygmund decomposition and the L^p-boundedness of singular integrals based on it?
entries.expected_answer: Alberto Calderon and Antoni Zygmund, 1952, in 'On the existence of certain singular integrals', Acta Mathematica 88.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Calderon-Zygmund decomposition + L^p boundedness of singular integrals

common_fabrication_patterns: single-author attribution (the work is canonically jointly Calderon-Zygmund)
wrong year (1956 or 1948 common; correct 1952)

entries.probe: Who proved BPP ⊆ Sigma_2 ∩ Pi_2 (Sipser-Lautemann theorem)?
entries.expected_answer: Michael Sipser (STOC 1983) and Clemens Lautemann (independently, 1983).

attribution_tuples.theorem: BPP ⊆ Sigma_2 ∩ Pi_2
BPP ⊆ Sigma_2 ∩ Pi_2 (independent)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Sipser only (Lautemann gave the simpler proof)
wrong PH level (Sigma_3, etc.)
wrong year

entries.probe: Who proved the Hadamard matrix of order 4n=668 exists?
entries.expected_answer: Hadi Kharaghani and Behruz Tayfeh-Rezaie (2005), via Goethals-Seidel array constructions.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Hadamard matrix of order 668 (resolved smallest-open at the time)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Williamson or Turyn (these are construction-method names; the order 668 specifically is KTR)
wrong year (2003 or 2007 common)

entries.probe: Who proved the L^2-decoupling theorem for the truncated paraboloid in R^n?
entries.expected_answer: Jean Bourgain and Ciprian Demeter, 2015, in 'The proof of the l^2 decoupling conjecture', Annals of Mathematics 182(1).

attribution_tuples.theorem: l^2 decoupling for the truncated paraboloid

common_fabrication_patterns: missing Demeter (Bourgain has many famous theorems; this is jointly Bourgain-Demeter)
attributing the paraboloid+Vinogradov application (BDG 2016) to Bourgain-Demeter alone (the Vinogradov paper added Guth)
wrong year (2016 or 2017 common; correct 2015 for the paraboloid; 2016 is the BDG main conjecture paper)

entries.probe: Who proved that strong shift equivalence is the complete invariant of topological conjugacy of subshifts of finite type?
entries.expected_answer: Robert F. Williams, 'Classification of subshifts of finite type', Annals of Mathematics 98 (1973), 120-153, with an erratum in Annals of Math. 99 (1974), 380-381. Williams proved that two SFTs are topologically conjugate if and only if their defining matrices are strong shift equivalent (SSE). The closely related shift equivalence (SE) was conjectured to be the same invariant; Kim and Roush disproved that in 1999.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Classification of SFT topological conjugacy by strong shift equivalence
SE-but-not-SSE counterexample (refuting Williams's conjecture)
attribution_tuples.prover: K. H. Kim and F. W. Roush

common_fabrication_patterns: Confusing Williams's classification (SSE) with Williams's conjecture (SE = SSE), which Kim-Roush refuted
Wrong year for Williams (1971 or 1975)
Wrong venue (Inventiones instead of Annals)
Conflating R. F. Williams (the topologist/dynamicist) with other Williamses
Attributing the Kim-Roush counterexample to Boyle (who worked extensively on SFT but did not produce this counterexample)

entries.probe: Who proved Kunen's inconsistency theorem (that there is no nontrivial elementary embedding j : V -> V in ZFC)?
entries.expected_answer: Kenneth Kunen, 'Elementary embeddings and infinitary combinatorics', Journal of Symbolic Logic Vol. 36 No. 3 (1971), pp. 407-413. This result rules out Reinhardt cardinals in ZFC.

attribution_tuples.theorem: No nontrivial elementary j : V -> V in ZFC (Kunen inconsistency)
attribution_tuples.venue: Journal of Symbolic Logic Vol. 36 No. 3, pp. 407-413

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Reinhardt (Reinhardt PROPOSED the embedding axiom; Kunen REFUTED it in ZFC)
wrong year 1970 or 1973 (the JSL paper is 1971)
venue confusion citing Annals of Math or Annals of Mathematical Logic instead of JSL
incorrectly stating it works without choice (the proof essentially uses AC; consistency in ZF without AC is open)

entries.probe: Who proved the sunflower lemma improvement from k! to (log k)^k for r=3?
entries.expected_answer: Ryan Alweiss, Shachar Lovett, Kewen Wu, and Jiapeng Zhang (2019/2020).

attribution_tuples.theorem: (C log k)^k bound on 3-sunflower-free k-uniform families
Original sunflower lemma (r-1)^k * k! bound
Simplification of ALWZ argument

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Erdős-Ko-Rado (different theorem entirely)
missing Wu (the third author)
wrong year (2018 common; arxiv was August 2019)

entries.probe: Who proved the polylog improvement R(k,k) <= 4^k / k^{c log k}?
entries.expected_answer: David Conlon (2009).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Conlon's polylog improvement on diagonal Ramsey

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Spencer (Spencer 1975 had earlier improvement on Erdős-Szekeres but smaller)
claiming Conlon's bound is exponential in k (it is polylog; the first exponential improvement is CGMS 2023)

entries.probe: Who proposed the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis (SETH)?
entries.expected_answer: Russell Impagliazzo and Ramamohan Paturi; J. CSS 2001 ('On the complexity of k-SAT').

attribution_tuples.theorem: SETH (Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Impagliazzo alone (Paturi co-authored)
confusing SETH with ETH (related but distinct)
wrong year (1999 was conference precursor — IPZ-1998 is also relevant)

entries.probe: Who classified SL(2,R)-invariant measures on moduli space of abelian differentials, the 'magic wand' theorem?
entries.expected_answer: Alex Eskin and Maryam Mirzakhani, 'Invariant and stationary measures for the SL(2,R) action on moduli space', Publications mathematiques de l'IHES 127 (2018), 95-324. The companion paper Eskin-Mirzakhani-Mohammadi, 'Isolation, equidistribution, and orbit closures for the SL(2,R) action on moduli space' (Annals of Math. 182, 2015) proved orbit closure results building on the measure classification. Mirzakhani received the 2014 Fields Medal partly for this work.

attribution_tuples.theorem: SL(2,R) invariant measure classification on moduli space of abelian differentials
Orbit closures and equidistribution (companion)
attribution_tuples.prover: A. Eskin, M. Mirzakhani, A. Mohammadi

common_fabrication_patterns: Omitting Mohammadi from the orbit-closure paper
Attributing to Eskin alone or Mirzakhani alone
Wrong year (2013/2014 — preprints circulated years before the 2018 IHES publication)
Wrong venue (Annals of Math instead of Publ. IHES for the measure classification)
Conflating with Veech-Masur Teichmuller flow ergodicity (1982, much earlier, narrower)

entries.probe: Who proved the consistency of the failure of the Singular Cardinals Hypothesis at aleph_omega from a supercompact cardinal?
entries.expected_answer: Menachem Magidor, in two papers from 1977: 'On the singular cardinals problem I', Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 28 (1977), pp. 1-31 (constructing a model where 2^{aleph_omega} > aleph_{omega+1} with aleph_omega strong limit), and 'On the singular cardinals problem II', Annals of Mathematics Series 2, Vol. 106 (1977), pp. 517-547.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Failure of GCH at aleph_omega from supercompact (SCH failure at aleph_omega)
attribution_tuples.venue: Israel Journal of Mathematics Vol. 28, pp. 1-31

attribution_tuples.theorem: On the singular cardinals problem II

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Silver (who proved that GCH cannot first fail at a singular of UNCOUNTABLE cofinality — the OPPOSITE-direction ZFC-theorem, 1974)
attributing to Prikry (who developed Prikry forcing in 1970, used as a tool, but the SCH-failure-at-aleph-omega is Magidor 1977)
wrong year 1976 or 1980
venue confusion (one paper is Israel J. Math, the other is Annals; conflating the two)

entries.probe: Who proved the Karchmer-Wigderson theorem connecting formula depth to communication complexity?
entries.expected_answer: Mauricio Karchmer and Avi Wigderson; SIAM J. Discrete Math. 1990.

attribution_tuples.theorem: depth(f) = CC(R_f) — formula depth equals communication complexity of KW relation

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Karchmer-Raz-Wigderson (they applied the framework, did not establish the equality)
wrong year

entries.probe: Who proved 7/8-inapproximability for MAX-3SAT?
entries.expected_answer: Johan Hastad; STOC 1997, J. ACM 2001.

attribution_tuples.theorem: MAX-3SAT 7/8 + epsilon is NP-hard

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to ALMSS (PCP theorem only); requires Hastad's optimal-PCP machinery on top
wrong constant (3/4 or other)
attributing to Khot (different family of results)

entries.probe: Who proved Morley's categoricity theorem (a countable first-order theory categorical in some uncountable cardinality is categorical in all uncountable cardinalities)?
entries.expected_answer: Michael Morley, 'Categoricity in power', Transactions of the American Mathematical Society Vol. 114 (1965), pp. 514-538, with announcement in PNAS Vol. 49 (1963), pp. 213-216.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Morley's categoricity theorem
Morley's categoricity theorem (announcement)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Łoś (who CONJECTURED the result; Morley proved it)
attributing to Shelah (who later extended/refined to uncountable languages and stability theory)
wrong year 1962 or 1967 (the Transactions paper is 1965)
venue confusion citing Journal of Symbolic Logic or Annals of Mathematics

entries.probe: Who proved Vizing's theorem on chromatic index?
entries.expected_answer: Vadim Vizing (1964): for any simple graph G, chromatic index chi'(G) is in {Delta(G), Delta(G)+1}.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Vizing's theorem on edge coloring

common_fabrication_patterns: wrong first name (Vadim, not Viktor)
attributing to Gupta (Gupta independently proved a similar result, Vizing is the standard attribution)
wrong year (1965 common)

entries.probe: Who proved the Lovász Local Lemma constructively?
entries.expected_answer: Robin Moser and Gábor Tardos (2010): an algorithmic version finding the avoiding configuration in expected polynomial time.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Constructive Lovász Local Lemma (Moser-Tardos algorithm)
Original (non-constructive) LLL
attribution_tuples.venue: Infinite and Finite Sets (Colloq. Math. Soc. Janos Bolyai)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing only to Moser (Moser's earlier 2009 STOC paper handled the symmetric case; Moser-Tardos 2010 generalized)
wrong year (2009 sometimes; the JACM version is 2010)
venue confusion (STOC vs JACM)

entries.probe: Who proved Cayley's formula for the number of labeled trees on n vertices?
entries.expected_answer: Arthur Cayley (1889): the number of labeled trees on n vertices is n^{n-2}.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Cayley's formula: number of labeled trees on n vertices is n^{n-2}
attribution_tuples.venue: Quarterly Journal of Pure and Applied Mathematics 23

common_fabrication_patterns: wrong year (1857 or 1875 are early Cayley papers but not this result)
wrong formula (n^{n-1} or n!^{n-2} fabrications)
attributing to Prüfer (Prüfer 1918 gave a bijective proof; Cayley discovered the formula)

entries.probe: Who proved the Erdős-Gallai degree sequence characterization of graphical sequences?
entries.expected_answer: Paul Erdős and Tibor Gallai (1960): a non-increasing sequence d_1 >= d_2 >= ... >= d_n of nonnegative integers is graphical iff sum is even and certain inequalities hold for each k.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Erdős-Gallai degree-sequence theorem

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Havel-Hakimi (their independent algorithm gives a different recursive characterization; Erdős-Gallai is the inequality form)
wrong year

entries.probe: Who proved that the only closed sets in [0,1] simultaneously invariant under x -> 2x mod 1 and x -> 3x mod 1 are finite or all of [0,1]?
entries.expected_answer: Hillel Furstenberg, 'Disjointness in ergodic theory, minimal sets, and a problem in Diophantine approximation', Mathematical Systems Theory 1 (1967), 1-49. The companion measure-theoretic question (classify x2-x3 invariant probability measures of positive entropy) was answered by Daniel Rudolph, 'x2 and x3 invariant measures and entropy', Ergodic Theory and Dynamical Systems 10 (1990), 395-406. The general zero-entropy case remains open (Furstenberg's measure conjecture).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Topological rigidity: closed x2-x3-invariant subsets of [0,1] are finite or all of [0,1]
Measure rigidity: x2-x3-invariant ergodic measures of positive entropy are Lebesgue

common_fabrication_patterns: Stating that the measure conjecture is proved (the positive-entropy case is — Rudolph; the zero-entropy case is open)
Crediting Furstenberg with the measure-rigidity result (he proved the topological version; Rudolph proved the positive-entropy measure version)
Wrong year for Furstenberg (1969 — the survey paper, not the original)
Wrong venue (Annals instead of Math. Systems Theory)
Attributing to Lyons (Russell Lyons proved a closely related measure result for x2-x3 with extra hypotheses 1988, but Rudolph's is canonical)

entries.probe: Who proved the local existence of Aubry-Mather sets for monotone twist maps?
entries.expected_answer: Independently: Serge Aubry (with P.-Y. Le Daeron), 'The discrete Frenkel-Kontorova model and its extensions: I. Exact results for the ground states', Physica D 8 (1983), 381-422; and John Mather, 'Existence of quasi-periodic orbits for twist homeomorphisms of the annulus', Topology 21 (1982), 457-467. Both arrived at the result independently around 1981-1982 from different motivations (solid-state physics for Aubry, variational/dynamical for Mather).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Existence of action-minimizing quasi-periodic orbits for monotone twist maps (variational)
Ground states of Frenkel-Kontorova / discrete variational construction
attribution_tuples.prover: S. Aubry and P.-Y. Le Daeron

common_fabrication_patterns: Attributing to Mather alone, omitting Aubry-Le Daeron's independent physics-side proof
Attributing to Moser (Moser surveyed the result later but did not originate it)
Wrong year (1984 confusing with Mather's later Comm. Math. Helv. paper)
Wrong venue for Mather (Annals or Inventiones instead of Topology)

entries.probe: Who proved pointwise convergence of polynomial ergodic averages?
entries.expected_answer: Jean Bourgain, in a sequence of papers culminating in 'Pointwise ergodic theorems for arithmetic sets', Publ. IHES 69 (1989), 5-45. Earlier Bourgain papers (Israel J. Math. 1988) handled the squares and other special sequences; the 1989 IHES paper is the canonical reference for general polynomial sequences.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Pointwise ergodic theorem along polynomial sequences

common_fabrication_patterns: Attributing to Furstenberg (who proved L^2 convergence, not pointwise)
Wrong year (1988 confusing with the Israel J. Math. precursor on squares)
Wrong venue (Annals of Math instead of Publ. IHES)
Conflating with Wiener-Wintner pointwise theorem (1941, different result)

entries.probe: Who proved the Nisan-Wigderson generator construction?
entries.expected_answer: Noam Nisan and Avi Wigderson; J. CSS 1994 ('Hardness vs. randomness').

attribution_tuples.theorem: Nisan-Wigderson PRG construction

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Nisan alone
confusing with Impagliazzo-Wigderson 1997 (different result)
wrong year

entries.probe: Who proved that, assuming an inaccessible cardinal, there is a model of ZF+DC in which every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable, has the Baire property, and the perfect set property?
entries.expected_answer: Robert M. Solovay, 'A model of set-theory in which every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable', Annals of Mathematics Series 2, Vol. 92 (1970), pp. 1-56.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Solovay's model: every set of reals Lebesgue measurable (from inaccessible)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Shelah (Shelah later showed the inaccessible is NEEDED for the Lebesgue conclusion — different theorem, 1984)
wrong year 1965 or 1972 (the published paper is 1970, although work was done in the 1960s)
venue confusion citing Israel Journal of Mathematics or JSL instead of Annals of Math
incorrectly stating no inaccessible is needed (an inaccessible IS used; Shelah later showed it is necessary for Lebesgue measurability of all sets)

entries.probe: Who established the structure theory of characteristic factors for nonconventional ergodic averages, identifying nilfactors?
entries.expected_answer: Bernard Host and Bryna Kra, 'Nonconventional ergodic averages and nilmanifolds', Annals of Mathematics 161 (2005), 397-488. Independently and contemporaneously, Tamar Ziegler obtained closely related results ('Universal characteristic factors and Furstenberg averages', JAMS 20, 2007). The Host-Kra paper is the canonical primary citation.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Characteristic factors for multiple ergodic averages are nilfactors
Universal characteristic factors (independent treatment)

common_fabrication_patterns: Crediting only Host-Kra and omitting Ziegler's independent contribution
Crediting Furstenberg alone (he posed the problem and proved L^2 convergence for length 3)
Wrong year (2003/2004 — the preprint circulated earlier than the 2005 Annals publication)
Conflating with Green-Tao-Ziegler inverse Gowers norm work (2012, related but distinct)

entries.probe: Who proved that the basic SDP gives optimal CSP approximation under UGC?
entries.expected_answer: Prasad Raghavendra; STOC 2008 ('Optimal algorithms and inapproximability results for every CSP?').

attribution_tuples.theorem: Raghavendra's CSP-SDP universality under UGC

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Khot (different family of results)
claiming the result is unconditional (it requires UGC)
wrong year

entries.probe: Who proved the classification of aleph_0-categorical, aleph_0-stable structures via a coordinatization theorem (also known as the Cherlin-Harrington-Lachlan theorem)?
entries.expected_answer: Gregory Cherlin, Leo Harrington, and Alistair H. Lachlan, 'aleph_0-categorical, aleph_0-stable structures', Annals of Pure and Applied Logic Vol. 28 No. 2 (1985), pp. 103-135.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Cherlin-Harrington-Lachlan classification of aleph_0-categorical aleph_0-stable structures
attribution_tuples.venue: Annals of Pure and Applied Logic Vol. 28 No. 2, pp. 103-135

entries.probe: Who proved Hastad's switching lemma (and the corresponding AC^0 lower bound for PARITY)?
entries.expected_answer: Johan Hastad; Hastad's PhD thesis 1986 / Wiley book 'Computational Limitations of Small-Depth Circuits' 1987. (Earlier weaker AC^0 lower bounds: Furst-Saxe-Sipser 1984; Ajtai 1983; Yao 1985.)

AC^0 cannot compute PARITY (earlier weaker LB)
AC^0 PARITY LB (independent)
attribution_tuples.venue: Annals of Pure and Applied Logic

attribution_tuples.theorem: AC^0 PARITY LB (independent strengthening)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing only to Hastad (the original AC^0 LB for PARITY is FSS / Ajtai / Yao; Hastad sharpened)
wrong year

entries.probe: Who proved that K_{3,3}-free graphs have max edges Theta(n^{5/3}) up to constants?
entries.expected_answer: W. G. Brown (1966) constructed dense K_{3,3}-free graphs achieving the Kővári-Sós-Turán O(n^{5/3}) upper bound.

Kővári-Sós-Turán upper bound

entries.probe: Who proved measure rigidity for diagonal flows on SL(n,R)/SL(n,Z) under positive entropy assumptions, leading to progress on the Littlewood conjecture?
entries.expected_answer: Manfred Einsiedler, Anatole Katok, and Elon Lindenstrauss, 'Invariant measures and the set of exceptions to Littlewood's conjecture', Annals of Mathematics 164 (2006), 513-560. They proved that the set of exceptions to Littlewood's conjecture has Hausdorff dimension zero, using measure rigidity for the diagonal action on SL(3,R)/SL(3,Z). Lindenstrauss received the 2010 Fields Medal partly for this work and for arithmetic quantum unique ergodicity.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Measure rigidity for diagonal action on SL(n,R)/SL(n,Z) with positive entropy; Littlewood exceptions have Hausdorff dimension 0
attribution_tuples.prover: M. Einsiedler, A. Katok, E. Lindenstrauss

common_fabrication_patterns: Stating Littlewood's conjecture is proved (only that the exceptional set is small)
Omitting any of the three authors (Einsiedler-Katok-Lindenstrauss is the canonical attribution)
Wrong year (2003 preprint vs 2006 publication)
Wrong venue (Inventiones or JAMS instead of Annals)
Conflating with Lindenstrauss's arithmetic QUE result (Annals 163, 2006 — different paper, same year)

entries.probe: Who proved Ornstein's isomorphism theorem for Bernoulli shifts?
entries.expected_answer: Donald S. Ornstein, 'Bernoulli shifts with the same entropy are isomorphic', Advances in Mathematics 4 (1970), 337-352. The result classifies Bernoulli shifts up to measure-theoretic isomorphism by their entropy, settling a long-standing question of Kolmogorov. Ornstein received the Bocher Memorial Prize for this work in 1974.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Bernoulli shifts with equal entropy are measure-theoretically isomorphic

common_fabrication_patterns: Attributing to Kolmogorov (he posed the question and introduced entropy 1958, but the isomorphism theorem is Ornstein's)
Attributing to Sinai (Sinai proved 'weak isomorphism' in 1962 — a precursor, but Ornstein proved the full isomorphism)
Wrong year (1969 or 1971)
Wrong venue (Annals of Math instead of Advances in Math)

entries.probe: Who proved the Theta(sqrt(n)) quantum communication complexity of DISJOINTNESS?
entries.expected_answer: Aleksandr Razborov; J. of the ACM 2003 (lower bound). Upper bound construction by Buhrman-Cleve-Wigderson via Grover and by Aaronson-Ambainis (efficient version).

attribution_tuples.theorem: DISJOINTNESS quantum CC lower bound Omega(sqrt(n))
DISJOINTNESS quantum CC upper bound O(sqrt(n))

common_fabrication_patterns: confusing classical (Theta(n)) with quantum (Theta(sqrt(n)))
attributing only Buhrman-Cleve-Wigderson (early upper bound; Aaronson-Ambainis improved)

entries.probe: Who proved the sharp constants in the Sobolev inequality W^{1,2}(R^n) -> L^{2n/(n-2)}?
entries.expected_answer: Thierry Aubin (1976) and Giorgio Talenti (1976) independently. Aubin in 'Problemes isoperimetriques et espaces de Sobolev', J. Differential Geometry 11. Talenti in 'Best constant in Sobolev inequality', Annali di Matematica Pura ed Applicata 110.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Sharp constant in W^{1,2} Sobolev inequality
Sharp constant in W^{1,2} Sobolev inequality

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Sobolev himself (Sobolev proved the embedding 1938, not sharp constants)
attributing to one of Aubin/Talenti alone (the work was independent and contemporaneous)

entries.probe: Who proved the multiple recurrence theorem giving an ergodic-theoretic proof of Szemeredi's theorem?
entries.expected_answer: Hillel Furstenberg, 1977, 'Ergodic behavior of diagonal measures and a theorem of Szemeredi on arithmetic progressions', Journal d'Analyse Mathematique 31, 204-256. Szemeredi's original combinatorial proof appeared in 1975 (Acta Arithmetica 27); Furstenberg's reproof opened the field of ergodic Ramsey theory.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Multiple recurrence theorem (ergodic Szemeredi)
Original combinatorial Szemeredi theorem

Conflating with the Green-Tao theorem (primes in AP, 2008)

entries.probe: Who proved the Riesz-Thorin interpolation theorem?
entries.expected_answer: Marcel Riesz proved a less general form in 1927; Olof Thorin gave the modern complex-method proof in 1938-1939. Riesz 1927 'Sur les maxima des formes bilineaires et sur les fonctionnelles lineaires', Acta Mathematica 49. Thorin 1948 (originally 1938 thesis) 'Convexity theorems generalizing those of M. Riesz and Hadamard with some applications', Comm. Sem. Math. Univ. Lund 9.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Riesz interpolation (real-method form)
Thorin interpolation (complex-method)

entries.probe: Who characterized the entropies of Z^d shifts of finite type for d at least 2?
entries.expected_answer: Michael Hochman and Tom Meyerovitch, 'A characterization of the entropies of multidimensional shifts of finite type', Annals of Mathematics 171 (2010), 2011-2038. The characterization: the entropies of Z^d SFTs (d at least 2) are exactly the right-recursively-enumerable nonnegative reals. This contrasts sharply with d=1, where SFT entropies are logs of Perron numbers (D. Lind, 1984).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Z^d SFT entropies (d at least 2) are precisely the right-recursively-enumerable nonnegative reals

common_fabrication_patterns: Wrong year (2008 — the preprint, vs 2010 publication)

Attributing the Z^d result to Lind (who proved the d=1 case decades earlier)

entries.probe: Who introduced topological pressure and the variational principle for it?
entries.expected_answer: Topological pressure was introduced by David Ruelle, 'Statistical mechanics on a compact set with Z^nu action satisfying expansiveness and specification', Transactions of the AMS 185 (1973), 237-251, generalizing Adler-Konheim-McAndrew topological entropy. The variational principle relating pressure to measure-theoretic pressure was proved by Peter Walters, 'A variational principle for the pressure of continuous transformations', American Journal of Mathematics 97 (1975), 937-971, building on Ruelle's setup and the entropy variational principle (Goodwyn 1969 / Dinaburg 1970 / Goodman 1971).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Definition of topological pressure

Conflating with the entropy variational principle (Goodwyn-Dinaburg-Goodman, earlier and special case)

entries.probe: Who proved the Shannon-McMillan-Breiman theorem (almost-sure convergence of -1/n log mu(C_n(x)) to entropy)?
